frameworks.name: policy compression
frameworks.one_line: capacity-limited agents trade reward for simpler action policies

frameworks.name: capacity-limited reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: RL with an information bottleneck on the state-to-action mapping

frameworks.name: reward-maximizing reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: unconstrained RL baseline cannot account for action bias

core_question: Do humans trade reward for simpler action policies as predicted by capacity-limited reinforcement learning?
key_assumptions: agents face a capacity limit on the state-action policy
cognitive cost scales with policy complexity
actions are decoded from a compressed code in a time-dependent manner
manipulations: redundancy of the reward-maximizing policy across states
key_findings: people exploit state-action-reward structure to compress policies
compressed policies are biased toward high marginal-probability actions, discarding state information
action bias grows with redundancy in the optimal policy and with memory load
time pressure further reduces policy complexity and increases action bias
interpretations: human choice balances reward maximization against a cost on policy complexity
alternative non-compression models fail to explain the observed action biases
